skill.name: task-estimation
skill.description: Estimate software development tasks accurately using various techniques. Use when planning sprints, roadmaps, or project timelines. Handles story points, t-shirt sizing, planning poker, and estimation best practices.
skill.summary: Apply agile estimation techniques including story points, planning poker, and t-shirt sizing for accurate sprint planning.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is purely documentation providing agile estimation templates and guidelines. Markdown code fences (```) were incorrectly flagged as shell execution. Reference URLs are documentation links, not network calls. No executable code, sensitive operations, or security risks present.

content.user_title: Estimate Development Tasks with Agile Methods
content.value_statement: Stop guessing task durations and start planning with confidence. This skill applies proven agile estimation techniques to help your team reach consensus on story points, identify risks early, and create realistic sprint plans.
actual_capabilities: Apply Fibonacci-based story point estimation (1-21 scale) with clear complexity guidelines
Facilitate planning poker sessions to achieve team consensus on task estimates
Convert between estimation formats including story points, t-shirt sizes, and time ranges
Adjust estimates based on risk factors and uncertainty using documented formulas
Generate structured estimation documents with acceptance criteria and breakdowns
Identify tasks requiring decomposition (13+ points) and suggest splitting strategies
limitations: Provides estimation frameworks only - does not track actual completion times or team velocity
Requires team participation for planning poker - cannot replace collaborative estimation sessions
Does not integrate with project management tools - outputs are markdown templates only
Estimation guidelines are generic - teams should calibrate scales to their own context

use_cases.title: Sprint Planning for Scrum Teams
use_cases.description: Guide your team through story point estimation during sprint planning. Use the Fibonacci scale to assess backlog items, facilitate planning poker for consensus, and generate structured estimation documents for each committed story.

use_cases.title: Roadmap Estimation for Product Managers
use_cases.description: Quickly size epics and features using t-shirt sizing for initial roadmap planning. Convert rough sizes to story points for capacity planning and identify which items need further decomposition before development.

use_cases.title: Risk-Adjusted Estimation for Technical Leads
use_cases.description: Apply uncertainty buffers to base estimates using the documented risk adjustment formula. Account for technical complexity, external dependencies, and unknown requirements when planning critical features.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Story Point Estimation
prompt_templates.prompt: Estimate the story points for this task using the Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21). Consider complexity, effort, and risk. Task description: [Insert task details here]

prompt_templates.title: Planning Poker Facilitation
prompt_templates.prompt: Facilitate a planning poker session for this user story. First, explain the story clearly. Then present the range of estimates from team members, identify the highest and lowest scores, and guide discussion to reach consensus. Story: [Insert user story]

prompt_templates.title: T-Shirt Size Conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these story point estimates to t-shirt sizes and estimated time ranges. Use the standard mapping where XS is 1-2 points and XL is 13+ points. Stories: [List stories with story points]

prompt_templates.title: Risk-Adjusted Estimation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate a risk-adjusted estimate for this task. Base estimate is [X] story points. Risk level is low/medium/high. Uncertainty is [X]%. Apply the adjustment formula with appropriate buffers for risk and uncertainty.

output_examples.input: Estimate a task to add user profile photo upload with image resizing
output: Breakdown: Frontend UI (2 points), Image processing logic (2 points), Testing (1 point)
Risks: File size validation, storage integration, security considerations

output_examples.input: Facilitate planning poker for a payment integration feature
output: Initial estimates range from 8 to 13 points
Discussion reveals: API complexity, compliance requirements, testing needs
Consensus reached: 13 points - recommend splitting into smaller stories
Suggested split: Payment provider selection (5), Core integration (5), Testing and compliance (3)

best_practices: Use relative estimation by comparing to known baseline stories rather than estimating in isolation
Always include the full team in estimation sessions - diverse perspectives improve accuracy
Apply uncertainty buffers (10-50%) to accounts for unknown requirements and technical risks
anti_patterns: Treating story points as hours or days - points measure relative complexity, not time
Allowing managers to dictate estimates - team must reach consensus without pressure
Estimating stories larger than 13 points without splitting - epics should be decomposed first

faq.question: What is the difference between story points and time estimates?
faq.answer: Story points measure relative complexity using the Fibonacci sequence. They account for effort, risk, and uncertainty. Time estimates (hours/days) often miss these factors and vary by developer skill level.

faq.question: How do we establish a baseline for story point estimation?
faq.answer: Select a simple, well-understood story from your backlog and assign it 2-3 points. Use this as your reference when estimating new stories by comparing relative complexity.

faq.question: What should we do when estimates vary widely during planning poker?
faq.answer: Have the highest and lowest estimators explain their reasoning. Often the high estimator sees complexity others missed, or the low estimator knows of a simpler approach. Discuss and re-vote.

faq.question: Can story points be compared across different teams?
faq.answer: No. Each team calibrates points based on their velocity and baseline stories. Comparing points across teams leads to inaccurate conclusions and unhealthy competition.

faq.question: How often should we revisit estimates during a sprint?
faq.answer: Estimates should remain stable during the sprint. If scope changes significantly, track it separately and discuss during sprint retrospective rather than mid-sprint re-estimation.

faq.question: What velocity should we use for our first sprint planning?
faq.answer: Use historical data if available. For new teams, estimate conservatively and treat the first 2-3 sprints as calibration. Average actual completed points to establish baseline velocity.
